comment.body:
This is the traditional definition of pinning from investopedia. 

"Pinning the strike is the tendency of an underlying security's market price to close at or very near to the strike price of heavily traded options (in the same security) as the expiration time nears. This doesn't always happen, but it is most likely to occur when there is significant open interest in a particular expiring option that is near the money."

comment.body:
TV chart: SPX - ES1!, with back-adjustment on for ES.
SPX Forward Prices are well above Spot, making longer-dated ATM Puts much cheaper than ATM Calls.
I.e. the 50-Delta Put/Call (~forward) prices are well above Spot.
This is probably based on short-term interest rates being much higher than dividend yields.
This may start to change as:
- interest rates are cut, and/or
- stock prices decrease, reducing denominator for dividend yields, and/or
- per-share dividend payouts increase.
The chart shows how this can change over time.
It may change if short-term rates start being cut in September, or rising long-term rates force up dividend yields.

comment.body:
//@version=6
indicator("ES_SPX_FwdDiff")
es_ticker = ticker.new("CME_MINI_DL","ES1!",session=session.regular,backadjustment=backadjustment.on)
es = request.security(es_ticker,timeframe.period,close)
spx = request.security("CBOE:SPX",timeframe.period,close)
spx_daily_ma = request.security("CBOE:SPX","D",ta.ema(close,20))
plot( ta.cum(ta.change(spx-es) / spx_daily_ma) )
comment.raw_body:
